text: [Autosport] Putting Lewis Hamilton's F1 career in perspective
label: technical

text: Lewis has been a part of 387 races since his debut in 2007 and he's won 106 of them despite being winless for 3 whole seasons. And he was 103/288 at the end of the 2021. Insanity.
label: technical

text: Truly mental : 387 races, 206 podiums, 106 wins. Can't figure which is the most phenomenal rate : 53% podium rate, 27% win rate. For reference, M. Schumacher stands at :307 races, 155 podiums (50%),91 wins (29%)
label: technical

text: even more insane is that he's been on podium for 206... over half of the races he's started (53%), he's been on the podium, even with the barren streak of recent years.
label: technical

text: Jim Clark was an MF monster. At the time of his death he had a career WIN percentage of over 35%. Thats pretty much unfathomable.
label: technical

text: Top 4 by win percentage after Barcelona are Clark (34.25%), Verstappen (29.58%), Schumacher (29.55%), Hamilton (27.39%). If Verstappen doesn't win in Austria, he'll fall back behind Schumacher. And if it weren't for the Mercedes comeback, Schumacher would sit at 36.4%. After the 2021 season and before his draught, Lewis was at 35.76%.
label: technical

text: Of the 22 current drivers combined careers, Hamilton has 39% of all the wins.
label: technical

text: Honda engines were decent in the last reg cycle and in 2021 though, with Red Bull winning 4 WDC’s and 2 WCC‘s nonetheless. You‘d think they learned one or two things and figured things out, so can‘t fully blame AM.
label: technical

text: Honda only had a small group in the UK, most of Honda's people worked in Japan. They were reshuffled at Honda, so they might have lost a few key people that fit better in their new positions, but they should still have most of their original bunch. Biggest issue for Honda was the decision to leave (so they weren't as involved in the shaping of the rules), and not having a team to work with until mid-2023. The other teams had a head-start on them. It doesn’t help that AM made late changes to the chassis that apparently meant the PU doesn’t intergrate as well.
label: technical

text: Ferrari brought new wheel rims to Barcelona, to manage rear tyre overheating on the SF-26 by the way of brake heat radiating through the rims and into the tyres, improving tyre degradation
label: technical

text: SF18H was quite an innovative car despite it falling behind Mercedes overall that year. The way they spent their development tokens for rear suspension upgrades improving the SF1000 to the SF21 was also remarkable. Pre TD39 SF75 was also an innovative design.
label: technical

text: Source. The wheel rims were manufactured by BBS Japan.This area of research is new for 2026, as the FIA mandated all wheel rims be the same for the Ground Effect era cars from 2022-2025. This year, however, they have removed restrictions and allowed teams to develop them in their unique way.
label: technical

text: Reminds me in 2018 the wheel rims were a relatively big place for innovation, especially with circuits like Sochi. I think Mercedes brought a set of rims that caused a hubbub around its legality for Sochi in 2018. I'm pretty sure they were unambiguously legal, and it was just over-enthusiastic press. At least with those cars and those tyres (which struggled a lot from thermal deg) during quali at Sochi you could only be that fast in 2 of 3 sectors, you either go hard in the first 2 and lose your tyres through the mickey mouse bits of the 3rd sector, or you save a little bit and finish stronger. Barcelona has had that under multiple different tyres, and it typically had it show up more in the race.
label: technical

text: Max Verstappen has just become the driver who has finished on the podium from the most different positions in the history of Formula 1 P1, P2, P3, P4, P5, P6, P7, P8, P9, P10, P14, P15, P16, P17, P18, P20 and the Pit Lane
label: technical

text: Reminder: grand slam, or grand chelem, is when the driver qualifies in pole position, wins the race, leads every lap, and sets the fastest lap. (I had to look it up)
label: technical

text: It’s required to fail shut. If it was doing a full rotation, then an actuator failure would see the wing keep spinning.
label: technical

text: It’s interesting because the technical regulations simply state that the rear wing flap must be controlled by the FIA ECU, have only two position (corner mode and straight line mode), actuate under 400ms and rotate about a single axis. There’s nothing to say the maximum angle change, only that the angle of incidence between flaps needs to decrease when going from cornering mode to straight line mode, and must return by the same magnitude when going back to cornering mode. If the angle of incidence between wing elements when closed is for instance +20 degrees, actuating the RW Flap by 180 degrees reduces this from +20 degrees to -160 degrees when measuring from the same point, so seems to technically be correct?
label: technical

text: “While Hamilton’s stint was brief, journalist Simone Peluso noticed an immediate uptick in the ‘first telemetry data’. He apparently gained ‘8-10km/h’ on the straights relative to the earlier spec.” Quote pulled from here
label: technical

text: How the hell they packaged the mechanism into those thin endplates? That's actually impressive packaging considering the forces it needs to withstand.
label: technical

text: McLaren’s best form for arguably 40 years as well. If Oscar starts a dynasty and starts winning titles back to back like Lewis did at Mercedes, it’ll be in the same ballpark legacy wise. Back in 2022 most people were less than optimistic for Alpine but McLaren seemed stuck in a loop of improving in season and then messing up the winter. The scale of McLaren’s rise was absolutely unexpected. Nobody would have bet they’d have won the WCC within 2 seasons and have the double in 3. Alpine’s downfall however was really inevitable.
label: technical

text: Jenson Button was the first driver to press the DRS button at the 2011 Australian GP. Fernando Alonso made the first DRS overtake in the Australian GP 2011 on Nico Rosberg. Kimi Antonelli is the last driver to press a DRS button.
label: technical

text: What is DRS and why does the device raise criticism?
label: technical

text: Formula1.com: Drivers can then switch to X-mode, which is a low-drag configuration that sees the flap angle change on both the front and rear wing to maximise straight-line speed. The system will be driver-activated and available in certain parts of the track where lower levels of downforce are safe. The FIA say that based on current discussions, they are anticipating it will be available for any straight line longer than three seconds.
label: technical

text: It is a per lap activation, so no zones. It is, however, restricted to high speeds due to the way it works. The maximum MGU-K power of these cars will be gradually reduced from 350kW to 0 with increasing speed to manage energy. Override mode overrides this drop-off and delays it. It usually starts at 290kph, override delays it to ~347 338kph, I think, and it hits 0 at 355kph instead of normally 345kph.
label: technical

text: Mercedes actually developed a double-DRS loophole in 2012, where when the rear wing flap is opened under DRS, it funnels an air through a small gap in the rear wing all the way to the front wing. When air hits the front wing, it stalls it and thereby further increasing top speed, and that's how they got the advantage to win China 2012. An ingenious device that got banned very early on. https://www.autosport.com/f1/news/banned-the-mercedes-f1-teams-double-drs-device-4982943/4982943/
label: technical

text: They will now have "Active Aero" for both front and rear wings which will open up the wings when using them. They will have a Z-MODE for using the active aero for cornering speeds. They will have a X-MODE which is low-drag meant to maximize straight-line speed.
label: technical

text: We get an override mode for an overtaking aid, they press a button and get additional power from the MGU when 1 second behind a car.
label: technical

text: Drag coefficients for Mercedes, Ferrari, and Red Bull in Bahrain quali
label: technical

text: Another thing to consider is that racing tyres at these temps tend to 'melt' or deform onto the track in a way, creating a larger contact patch than it would otherwise be. This is a phenomenon known as 'stiction' (think sticky friction), and has a much greater resistance to rolling than a normal road tire.
label: technical

text: Can we have a discussion about the drag strip outside the final corner?
label: technical

text: The Rally car is made to get maximum grip in the slippery conditions. It’s geared really low for acceleration but low top end speed. Most Rally courses don’t have long straights, but a lot of corners. The F1 car has launch assist, but you have to wonder if that was dialed down with an inexperienced driver, even though it is Stig. Plus F1 cars struggle at slow speed with lack of downforce, but once it starts going faster, the air will push down on the car causing more grip and allowing it to handle better. The Formua E car is just pure garbage lol
label: technical

text: "but slip stream behind him could be crucial" Precisely what I think will happen as I've seen it happen all year. Max could have the speed advantage but the mcl has better corner speed and the drivers manage their tires really good. If they can hang onto him in the beginning or a restart, few corners then a straight and they're in the slipstream + DRS and he can get overtaken. Max will probably yield to maintain better pace than what he could have from defending and to avoid getting a happy little accident and few penalty points/+10 seconds.
label: technical

text: Will a lower down force setting increase degradation?
label: technical

text: Yes but it's manageable at Monza. If you have better straight line speed you can keep cars behind you as long as you don't mess up parabolica - we've seen this repeatedly in recent years. This goes 2x if the car is front is Max Verstappen with nothing to lose and the car behind is a McLaren which can't afford a DNF in a tight title fight.
label: technical

text: Stupid question! Usually teams are forced to declare new parts when it is the first time they are used in the season (this is why we have a list of upgrades usually published at every grand prix weekend). If you then take a buffer wheel at a part to modify it, are you supposed to publish again since technically it is a different part that you never used before?
label: technical

text: As the ground effect-era comes to an end, a look at the cars which adopted a higher-set front wing.
label: technical

text: Not sure I agree that the ground effect era is coming "to an end". The new regulations mean that the ground effect will be less powerful, sure, but we're not going back to flat floors. There will still be tunnels etc. Ground effect will still have a significant role to play in car performance. The big difference is that porpoising should become a much reduced problem, and obviously the new active aero that replaces DRS will be important to get right.
label: technical

text: To be fair the "ground effect" era really ended with TD39.
label: technical

text: We’ll see what happens. It could get interesting though. Anti-competitive practices against other businesses are taken quite seriously. In this case F1 officially said they would consider new team entries if they met their bar. If I’m recalling correctly, the first denial was for specific deficiencies in the team. But the latest denial is the problem. If Andretti failed to deliver and the denial was on technical reasons, then F1’s fine. But if Andretti checked all the boxes, and F1 denied them anyway, F1 is going to be in extremely hot water. Like “let Andretti in, or you have to pay them every cent that they’ve invested in your bad faith scheme”.
label: news_drama

text: They met the requirements for the FIA but apparently didn’t have the numbers for F1 even though they clearly said they did. I’m pretty sure the only thing F1 could argue is that Andretti wouldn’t bring any benefit to F1.
label: news_drama

text: [Chris Medland] BREAKING: Audi confirms Jonathan Wheatley will depart the team with immediate effect "due to personal reasons", with Mattia Binotto taking on team principal responsibilities. Wheatley has been heavily linked with the team principal role at Aston Martin
label: news_drama

text: It might be a location thing at least in part too, Wheatley was probably expected to move his life/family to Switzerland, where Audi operate their F1 team from, aka the old Sauber facilities, which is a big upheaval compared to moving from RBR in Milton Keynes to AM at Silverstone.
label: news_drama

text: Max Verstappen: "Apologies offered to Piastri, that's the most important thing" | Max Verstappen immediately apologized to Oscar Piastri after the Abu Dhabi Grand Prix: "He is a friend of mine, so I don't want to have any strange feelings going into the winter break."
label: news_drama
